title: Polymer clay brooch
subtitle: A textured oval brooch baked hard and fitted with a pin back
excerpt: A polymer clay brooch is cut from a rolled slab, textured with stamps or leaves, baked at 130°C for 20 minutes, and fitted with a glued-on pin back once cool. The whole process takes under an hour. Polymer clay does not need a kiln: the home oven does the curing. The brooch is lightweight and durable.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay brooch technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: is well suited to jewellery because it bakes hard without a kiln, takes surface texture well, and is light enough that a small brooch exerts no pull on fabric.
takes 20 minutes at 130°C. The pin back is attached with strong adhesive after baking, no metalworking required.

attrs.body: Brooch pin backs are available from craft suppliers. Use a two-part epoxy adhesive or a dedicated strong glue to attach the pin back to the cured brooch. Allow the adhesive to cure fully (at least two hours) before wearing. Do not bake the pin back inside the clay, metal backs can discolour the clay.
attrs.title: Pin backs

items.name: Polymer clay, one or two colours
Stamps, leaves, or lace for texture
Brooch pin back and strong adhesive

content.text: Condition the clay until pliable. Roll to 4 mm thick. For an oval brooch, cut an oval approximately 6 x 4 cm with the craft knife.

Press a stamp, leaf, or piece of lace into the surface of the brooch with firm even pressure. The impression should be clear and uniform across the surface. Press in a single firm push rather than rocking, which blurs the edges of the impression.

Run a finger along the cut edge of the brooch to smooth and slightly round it. A sharp flat edge can feel uncomfortable when worn against fabric. An even slight chamfer around the back perimeter is enough.

Place the brooch face-up on baking paper on a dedicated tray. Preheat the oven to 130°C, confirmed with the oven thermometer. Bake for 20 minutes. Remove and allow to cool completely on the tray, do not put cold water on hot polymer clay, as rapid cooling can cause cracks.

Once completely cool and hard, apply a small amount of strong adhesive to the pin back and press it onto the upper third of the brooch back. Hold in position for the time specified by the adhesive. Allow to cure fully before wearing.
Use a cookie cutter to cut a specific shape: a star, moon, or simple flower.
Apply millefiori cane slices to the raw brooch surface before baking for a patterned face.

attrs.intro: Problems when making the polymer clay brooch.

items.fix: Use a two-part epoxy adhesive rather than a standard craft glue. Allow the brooch to cool fully before gluing, the adhesive bonds better to a fully cold surface. Re-apply with fresh adhesive.
items.cause: Wrong adhesive, or adhesive applied before the brooch was completely cool.
items.symptom: Pin back falls off after a day of wear

items.fix: Bake face-up on flat baking paper. If the brooch is warping upward at the edges, weight the corners with small pieces of unbaked clay while baking. Remove the weights and bake for a final 5 minutes uncovered.
items.cause: Baked on a flat surface so only one face was in contact with the tray.
items.symptom: Brooch warps into a curve when baked

attrs.heading: Common problems
